expectations.evidence: Register access macros use volatile: '#define UART_SR (*(volatile uint32_t *)(UART_BASE + UART_SR_OFFSET))' and similarly for DR, BRR, CR1, CR2, CR3. rx_buffer, rx_head, and rx_tail are also declared volatile.

Calculates USARTDIV = 84,000,000 / (16 * 115,200) = 45.572916, Mantissa = 45, Fraction = 9, BRR = 0x2D9. States actual baud ~115,246 with ~0.04% error. The compute_brr() function uses integer arithmetic with rounding.

Uses ring buffer: 'static volatile uint8_t rx_buffer[RX_BUFFER_SIZE]', 'static volatile uint16_t rx_head', 'static volatile uint16_t rx_tail', with RX_BUFFER_MASK and bitmask indexing: '(rx_head + 1U) & RX_BUFFER_MASK'.
Uses C99 fixed-width types (uint8_t, uint16_t, uint32_t), bool from stdbool.h, and macros for register access. The compute_brr() function is C-style. No C++ features are used.
ISR reads DR: 'uint8_t data = (uint8_t)(UART_DR & 0xFFU)'. Handles overrun by also reading DR to clear: '(void)UART_DR' when ORE is set.
All memory is statically allocated: 'static volatile uint8_t rx_buffer[RX_BUFFER_SIZE]', static indices. No malloc, free, or new/delete anywhere in the driver.
No _Static_assert is used to validate buffer size power-of-2 constraint. The comment says 'Must be a power of 2 for efficient masking' but no compile-time assertion enforces this requirement.
Explicitly calculates: 'Actual baud = 84,000,000 / (16 * 45.5625) = 84,000,000 / 728.9999 = 115,246 (~0.04% error, well within UART tolerance).'

The ISR reads DR when RXNE is set, but does not explain WHY reading DR clears RXNE. There is no explanation of the hardware auto-clear mechanism. Note 4 mentions ring buffer power-of-2 but not the RXNE clear mechanism.
